sentences.text: [TEST PATTERN BEEPING]
I keep it?
Leon Borovick, L-E-O-N B-O-R-O-V-I-C-K.
I don't-- I cannot spell it.
And I--
It's Leyb, Leybl, Leybl.
Leyb.
And the last name?
Borowick, with a-- with a W.
With a W instead of a V.
Yeah.
In Polish, a W is pronounced like a V in English.
I forgot. 84, I think.
What is the city of your birth, the city and country?
Porzecze, near Grodno.
P-O-R-Z-E-C-Z-E.
My mother and--
Necha.
N-E-C-H-A.
Joseph.
My brother was with me together.
My brother was-- his name is Moishe.
Is he--
Younger than me.
Younger than you.
And who else was in your family?
I had three stepbrothers.
I had a sister.
I had a sister too.
My sister perished in camp.
Sarah.
Sarah, yeah.
What do you mean?
My father died when I was a year and a half old, he drowned.
Year and a half old.
Half and a year-- [LAUGHS]
A year and a half.
Years old, yeah.
We were in the-- in-- in-- in-- in the war.
In the war.
So you never really remember him.
My actual birth was in 1912.
My actual birth.
But in Germany I made myself younger in order to survive.
So I switched-- I came to America, I thought I had to transfer the same-- the same date.
Officially, I am 1914.
My father died when I was a year and a half.
She remarried.
I can't remember.
About six or five.
I can't remember.
Judel, Judel, Jude, Judel.
Judah, I don't know.
Segal.
Yeah.
Oy.
Do I have to give all these things?
The-- the-- the oldest was Motke and then Hirshke, Awremel, Schlemke, and a sister, Necha.
When I was very young, we were very, very poor, extremely poor.
Very young.
But then-- then my mother managed to [INAUDIBLE] us.
We-- we did make a living.
But I-- I was in cheder, in-- in an old-fashioned school until 12-- 12 years of age I didn't have any secular education.
We had-- we had only cheder, we had only arithmetic.
I remember Russian a little. 12-- when I was 12 years old, I-- I was in second grade, only in a secondary-- an elementary school.
Was unusual?
No, it was not.
It was unusual for the-- in-- for the-- for the-- as a Polish citizen it was unusual.
The Polish people, when they were 12 years old, they-- they used to be already in the seventh grade or the 15th grade.
But Jewish people were in cheder.
They didn't go to school.
Yeah, yeah.
Jewish children did not attend--
We didn't go.
We went to cheder.
Jewish children did not attend--
No.
No, the public-- the public schools.
But then it was compulsory.
So we had to go.
So I went-- I went to the 12th-- the 12th grade.
And I left my little town.
I went to a little-- little town, Jeziory.
[INAUDIBLE] And now you learn privately.
And I-- I entered the Zionist school.
It was called [POLISH], [POLISH].
I went in the seventh grade.
About 14 years old, I think.
I don't remember where.
I was there for a year.
Only in public school when I was there for less than a year in-- in my little town.
Yeah.
Very fast, yes.
Non-Jewish.
And then I went to-- in to that [POLISH].
We had only-- only Jewish children, Zionist school.
The language and instruction was Hebrew.
All subjects were on Hebrew except for Polish and, I think, in Polish history or something.
No, no.
My brother went to yeshiva for a short time.
Yes, certainly.
I-- my mother sent me to a business school.
I was up-- I graduated.
I was out two years.
It is in Polish, called [POLISH], you call it [POLISH] school.
To be a bookkeeper, actually, towards a diploma.
When I graduated, I was doing some bookkeeping, but I was fired.
I was not a-- I was a lousy bookkeeper.
It was not my-- my-- my talent for that.
I didn't have a talent for that.
I don't remember. 17 or 16?
I don't-- I can't remember.
Did many things.
She was-- she had-- she was-- she used to, you know, dye the garments in like a-- it's not painting.
You know, I told you about that.
She dyed the-- the fabrics.
Yeah.
Fabrics for the peasants.
And then she opened a store.
And she did that.
She did that.
She also was-- she was also a baker.
She baked bread and she sold it in the store.
A blacksmith.
Financially, it was-- we managed somehow to live, but my stepfather was not a-- the relations between us and the stepfather was not-- not a close relationship.
But his-- his-- his children were very close to us.
And I wrote a poem about these three brothers, my three stepbrothers who all died in-- in Auschwitz.
They were like my brothers.
The same thing.
We had the same feeling like it was my brother.
In-- yeah.
There were three brothers there.
In that poem?
Yeah.
I have-- have to get it.
Yeah.
Maybe I remember it by heart, I think.
Yeah, I think so.
I'll-- I'll try.
Should I-- should I recite now?
[POLISH]
No, no.
I'm mixed up.
OK.
What-- what-- which you're paying--
If I can take this-- can I take this for-- I have the poem.
Should I read it?
OK, a little bit later, yeah.
I mixed up the brothers.
The whole thing.
I have to read, later, the whole thing.
Very much, yeah, very much.
When my stepfather died, we-- my mother was traditional.
She was religious, but not-- not-- not-- not too pious-- not too religious.
I mean, not fanatic.
She-- she only used to go to shul about twice a year or three times, that's all.
When we-- when we were very later, when we were all grownups already-- I was 18, 19-- we seldom went to shul.
For the-- the high holidays.
Only in [INAUDIBLE], maybe.
But then I became-- I became a teacher.
And I was a-- a private teacher.
I was in-- in-- in-- in 1937.
In '38, in a little town of Sidra, near-- also not that far from Bialystok.
Sidra.
I had a-- I was teaching about nine or 10 girls the Hebrew language.
It was a Zionist group.
And I also asked private-- private lessons.
That's how I made my-- made my living.
And that's where I met my-- my actual wife.
It was Bashe.
She was called Bashe.
That's how-- that's how she is in my poems.
Vinick.
Yes.
Interesting.
I-- I-- I was the first-- the first Zionist in my little town when I was very young.
Then-- then I wasn't a happy Zionist.
From-- from reading.
From-- to ask-- sure, that was in the air.
The idea of-- of-- there was Keren Kayemet, Jewish National Fund.
So I used to get out-- to-- to collect money for the-- for that fund.
I didn't get paid for that.
I used-- it was in every house there was a pushka, you know, that little-- and we used to-- and then we used to go every week or so and collect the money.
That was a-- that was a tradition.
It was not Zionism.
It was-- that was Jewish life, a tradition to-- to-- to-- for Israel, for Zion.
No.
Unfortunately, I didn't have any plans then to go to Israel.
Even--
I believed in Zionism, that's right, yes.
No.
Yeah, yeah, for me too.
But see, to-- that would be to be uprooted from my-- my town.
We didn't think in these terms.
We just-- we were trying to help-- to do-- to help that cause.
But that [INAUDIBLE] didn't go.
We knew about that Dr.
Herzl, we heard about this.
I used to-- that's how I started writing poems.
I-- I published my first five poems in Philadelphia.
I sent from Poland in '98.
In '98, I sent five poems to-- and they published it in a-- in a Jewish newspaper.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH].
Over
Yeah.
No.
No.
Hitler was not in the picture then, when I started to-- to work for Keren Kayemet, for the Jewish National Fund.
It was later.
It was about maybe, I don't know, 17 years old or-- or 19.
I can't-- I don't remember.
I cannot say right.
Yeah.
No, no, no.
As I said, because to me-- see, the motive to me to collect money for-- for-- for Keren Kayemet, that-- that was the thing to do.
We didn't-- we didn't use the word 'Zionism' even then.
Didn't call ourselves Zionists.
I think that as a Jew I had to do that.
But later I-- I-- I-- I-- I learned, I read the Zionist literature.
Culture And-- but then I-- I was under the influence of the-- of the leftist organizations.
I was-- I was actually-- in my little town 2/3 of my life in Europe, I mean, in Poland, I-- I lived in-- I did live in my little town.
The rest I did spend in other towns.
I used to-- to-- I went to a little town near Jeziory to see [INAUDIBLE] I was in [POLISH], a few-- few places.
I-- I-- now when I look back in retrospect, I see that-- I feel that we were actually-- we were second-rate or third-rate citizens then.
There were no Jewish-- Jewish clerks in the post office or in the-- somewhere there was no police, there was no-- we had-- we had one Jewish policeman.
Doiste ?] was a volunteer in the Polish army against the saboteurs.
He was an exception.
So Jewish people lived, they were-- they were-- they had trades, blacksmiths and tailors and stores.
But we didn't have-- people didn't have any jobs, government jobs.
So it was-- it was tough to make a living.
And on this-- under these circumstances, the many-- there was an organization, the Bund, a Socialist Jewish party.
It was very, very influential in Poland, very popular.
And that was under-- under the influence of the-- of the literature on Marxism.
And then I-- then I-- I would say I was disappointed in all these things.
When did you become disappointed?
When did you become disillusioned?
When I read the-- the-- the trials in Soviet Russia, I guess.
The-- the Trotskyites, all the-- the-- all of the opponents of Stalin.
So then I said, must be something wrong with that.
About that, I remember once with a conversation with my brother, he said there was anti-Semitism in my town.
I myself didn't experience.
I didn't-- it was not such-- I didn't experience any persecution or something in Poland.
But-- but I felt we lived a different life.
We were isolated, Jewish people were.
We lived-- we-- we-- we spoke a different language.
We-- life was based on religious customs and all these things.
I forgot, what was the topic?
Acts of-- Anti-Semitism.
Yes.
Did you feel that the-- that the isolation was inflicted upon you?
Or did you feel that it was self--
No, the--

The people were-- people were polite and we'd actually-- my mother actually was born in a village, and the best-- our best-- when I was about six years old, five years old, our best friend was a Jewish peasant, a Jewish lady-- a non-Jewish lady.
The name by [?
Jurza. ?] We called her aunt, Aunt [?
Jurza. ?] And so my mother spoke perfect white Russian.
She was a blond.
And she mingled with the-- with the-- with peasant people, with ladies.
And so and she was liked by-- by the-- by the population, by the non-Jewish, the peasants.
They used to buy the-- what do you-- what do you say it in English?
The dye that you-- that you're dying, that--
But there was-- there was a very subtle hostility.
In school, in the public school, we all sometimes from the-- from the Polish boys, Christ killer.
You're a Christ killer.
And then let's say, once a year, when they used to go from the church with the ceremony, they called it, you know when the-- the-- birthday of Jesus.
What do you call that-- that-- that name?
Yeah, yeah.
So people didn't go out of the-- of the home.
They used to cover the-- the-- the icons in the streets.
So people used to stay home then.
I used to lay out the ground sometimes.
It was-- I-- I-- I-- I brought myself to do that.
But we knew there was a limit.
We can-- we didn't want to provoke them by-- by-- by being seen.
So that--
On the street, on the street.
No, there was no violence--
--in my little town.
When I was in Poland, no, not officially.
There was no violence.
No, peaceful.
I'd sit with a friend-- a friend, a non-Jewish friend, and the peasants used to buy in my mother's store.
But I didn't-- I didn't-- there was no violence.
There was no crime, even.
Not even-- I don't remember any crime in-- in my little town, even in the Polish population.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
That-- that we felt in our bones.
Because we couldn't-- people couldn't have a job-- couldn't have government jobs.
The rabbi and the cantor lived from the-- made a living out of the Jewish people, from the Jewish people.
There were-- there were--
There were Jewish lawyers and doctors.
And in my little town, there was not even one Jewish lawyer or-- or-- or doctor.
There were doctors in Poland, lawyers.
But it is known in the universities there was anti-Semitism.
There used to be not Jews-- Jews used to sit on different-- on different benches.
It's almost like the black people in America.
That was in the-- in the big town.
But in little towns we didn't-- that's interesting people don't know that.
My town was an exceptional little town.
We were a minority.
In my town, Porzecze, the Jewish people were a minority, a small minority.
That was the only-- the only little town, shtetl, that I know of with a Jewish minority.
In other little towns around Porzecze, the-- the-- the shtetlekh consisted of Jews.
Most of them, the Jews were in the town.
The non-Jews lived outside the town, the suburbs.
But there was one-- one little town, Sidra, where I was teaching, that it was like a Jewish-- like-- like-- like-- I felt like I was in a Jewish land.
There were only Jews there.
Only once a week they used to come to the market, the peasants, to sell things.
But otherwise, we were there.
We were used to-- to-- to-- to work down the streets about 11 o'clock, 10 o'clock at night, and only Jews were there.
It was actually an autonomy, in a way, in a culture.
Even in-- Grodno, in a big town, where gymnasiums, Jewish places, everything was in Hebrew, the schools.
See, I was teaching privately, even in my little town too, privately.
I didn't have formal education as a teacher.
And when you asked about [NON-ENGLISH], about future, we didn't think in these terms.
Now I'm-- I'm-- I'm reliving that-- that fear, what would I do if I grew up later?
I didn't have any-- any profession.
Yeah, but when the Soviets came, I became a teacher.
In-- in 1938, I-- I was working in a little-- in that Sidra, in that-- that little town.
When I came in the year 1939, the service took over.
So I was in my little town already.
And then-- and then they opened classes for-- for people who wanted to be teachers.
And so I volunteered.
And I graduated from my class in six months, a very intensive class.
Like ulpan, and the method of teaching was excellent, I must say this, in-- in-- in-- in that-- in that school we learned Russian, Russian geography and biology and-- and-- we learned a lot.
Took me six months.
So then I chose to be a teacher in-- in-- in-- near Sokolka, in a little village.
I wanted to be far from-- they offered me a job in my little town.
But I-- I begged them not to send me there because I didn't-- I wanted to be near my Bashe.
And they showed me the maps, the little-- little villages where I went to work.
So I-- I appointed to that place near her.
I was there.
I was teaching the whole year.
And every Sunday-- we got Sundays to come for the weekend to my-- to-- to her in Sokolka.
Cult
In 1941-- in 1941, I-- I-- I left for vacation to my little town.
So when I left that-- that house, I have Bashe there.
She insisted I stay her-- with her.
And I insisted that she comes with me.
So we were about discussing that what to do.
And then she accompanied me to the train, and the train, she said to me, and OK, come-- come to me, or come-- come with me.
I said, no, come with me.
And then the-- the-- that the men said, OK, we are moving.
And the train moved, and I went to my little town.
And then at-- at night, I had a dream that war broke out.
I had a dream that war broke out.
And I woke up about-- in the morning, I woke up and Mother came to my bed and said, says, Leybl, [NON-ENGLISH].
She said, [NON-ENGLISH] It's a word.
So I went to my-- to my neighbor and I heard the-- the-- the-- the radio.
The-- and the radio a voice of Soviet Russia, their representative, that war broke out and Hitler attacked our homeland.
No.
At that time there was no-- there was no transportation.
Train didn't run, and there was chaos.
And the-- the-- the Russians escaped in big trucks.
Yes.
We knew that about-- we knew about.
And I was-- I was prepared.
I knew-- I knew-- I knew that it would be a disaster.
Some people didn't believe me.
I was-- they said I was a pessimist, they used to call me in my little town.
They thought, one of the tragedies of the Jewish people was actually disbelief.
Many people, unfortunately, Jewish people, didn't believe that Hitler would do that, that the Germans would exterminate the Jewish people.
I was-- I was one of them who did believe.
I said-- I said [NON-ENGLISH].
It's bad.
It will be-- it will be a year in destruction.
Hitler said in And I read-- I heard his-- his-- his-- speeches.
He was like a-- like a madman he spoke.
Because I knew about, I read about-- when Hitler sent out many Jews from Germany to a little camp [POLISH] in Poland.
And then the Jews told-- told the people, the Jewish people, how life in Germany, persecution of the Jewish people.
And I even wrote a poem at that time about that, but they never published it.
And then I remember when I heard on the radio, I came to my mother, I said, Mom, you had [INAUDIBLE].
I'm running.
Told her, I have to go to Soviet Russia.
I am running.
She gave me bread and some, I think, underwear.
And I was-- I left my house.
It was in the evening.
And I look back, I say, how could I-- how could I know I have to go to Russia?
How can I know the direction?
But I came to Jeziory, where my family was.
And from there, I and my cousin, we-- we left in a group of young people.
What was your cousin's name?
Bill, [?
Berele. ?] His name was [?
Berele. ?]
Borowick also.
A very talented-- talented young man, very talented.
He would have been a scientist now, I would think.
